title: Vat sizing, sizing handmade sheets for writing and printing
subtitle: How to treat freshly pulled sheets so ink does not feather or bleed
excerpt: Freshly pulled handmade paper is water-leaf: fully absorbent, with no sizing. It feathers badly when written on with a dip pen. Vat sizing adds gelatin or starch to the sheet during or after forming so that it accepts ink without bleeding into the fibre.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/oldpapermaking00hunt. Discusses sizing practice in historical Western hand papermaking. Surface sizing with gelatin is documented across multiple public-domain papermaking manuals of the late Victorian and Edwardian period.

glossaryTerms.definition: An unsized handmade sheet with no gelatin or starch treatment. Fully absorbent; ink feathers badly on water-leaf paper.

Applying a gelatin or starch solution to the surface of a dried sheet to reduce absorbency. Distinct from internal sizing (adding rosin or other materials to the vat pulp), which is more complex at home scale.

content.text: Every freshly pulled handmade sheet starts as
: completely unsized, every fibre exposed, ready to absorb any liquid at once. On water-leaf paper, a calligraphy nib produces a feathered, spreading line rather than a clean edge.
reduces this absorbency by partly filling the spaces between fibres with a gelatin or starch film.

attrs.title: Safety: hot gelatin near water
attrs.body: The gelatin sizing bath is prepared at 60°C. Use a thermometer to monitor temperature. Work away from the papermaking vat water source to prevent cold-water splashes into the hot bath. Do not lean over the bath while stirring.

items.name: Leaf gelatin, food-grade (slug: gelatin-leaf)
items.description: 250-bloom platinum grade. Dissolve in hot water (60°C) at a concentration of 2 g per litre. This is a light sizing for calligraphy; increase to 4 g per litre for printing papers.

items.name: Tray larger than the sheets being sized
items.description: A roasting tray or similar, filled to a depth of 30 mm with the gelatin sizing bath.

items.name: Dried, pressed handmade sheets
items.description: Sheets must be fully dry and flat before surface sizing.

items.name: Press boards (slug: papermaking-press-boards)
items.description: For pressing the sized sheets flat while drying.

items.name: Papermaking sponge (slug: papermaking-sponge)
items.description: For blotting excess sizing before re-pressing.

content.text: Soak the gelatin leaves in cold water for 5 minutes until soft. Squeeze out the excess water. Dissolve in a small amount of hot water (60°C) by stirring. Add the dissolved gelatin to the sizing tray filled with water at approximately 40°C. Stir to combine. The bath should feel slightly slippery between the fingers; if it feels like plain water, dissolve a second leaf.

Slide one dry sheet flat into the sizing tray, held at a diagonal to let air escape as the sheet enters. Allow it to absorb the sizing solution for 30 seconds, then lift it by two adjacent corners. Let most of the liquid drain back into the tray. Place the sheet face down on the press boards, cover with a second board, and stack additional sheets above (each with their press board between).

Once all sheets are dipped, press the full stack firmly to drive out excess sizing. Weight with heavy books or use the book press for 30 minutes. The sheets will stick to each other slightly as the gelatin dries; separate them carefully while still damp and hang or lay flat to finish drying. Do not force separate a fully dry gelatin-bonded stack: re-dampen with a mist of water first.

Once dry, test with a dip pen and iron-gall ink. Write a few letters and assess the line edges. The ink should stay within the stroke and dry without spreading. If the edges still feather, repeat the sizing with a slightly stronger bath (3 g gelatin per litre) and dry again.
